## [00:00 - 03:03]

In this lesson, you'll learn all the different types of shipping terms and how is that going to change your cost of goods sold. And it's super important because sometimes you're getting a quote or something cheaper, it might not be cheaper in the end. So we're going to go through all the details in this lesson. The first one is I want to quickly talk about, I think what we might have mentioned in the previous lessons is that COGS. What is COGS? COGS is basically the abbreviation for cost of goods sold. So it's the direct cost achievable to the production of the goods sold by you. It also includes all the input freight, which means all the international freight and duty if applicable. And then we talk about training terms. There are a lot of different training terms, but there are three terms that are more or less relatable to you guys. The first one is EXW called XWORKS. So what XWORKS is, is that it's basically the products, when product finished, that's the cost, how much it would be for you to transport from the factory all the way to your warehouse at destination. So this is basically what it is. The second one is FOB. It's basic stands for free on board. The factory will include cost from the factory to the port. So you don't need to cover the transportation of that section. All you need to do is you pay the ocean freight from the port, origin port to destination, all the way to your warehouse. The third one is called CIF or CNF. Basically is a cost of freight included. What that means is that you have production finished, the factory will actually look after the ocean freight for you all the way to your destination port. Under EXWWORKS, the stock basically is located in the factory and full payment needs to be made before you can move any stock. And you have to organize a freight forwarder to pick up the stock from the factory and move all the way to your warehouse. And under FOB, because the factory look after local transportation at our region, all you need to do is basically ask the freight forwarder to pick up the container from the port and all the way to your warehouse. And under CIF or CNF, the container is basically shipped to your destination port. You just need to organize a custom broker to do custom clearance for you and transportation company to move the cargo from the port to your warehouse. Simple as that. That's the three major trading term that are going to be relatable to you. And we're going to talk about how that affects your cost of goods sold.

## [03:03 - 06:08]

So under EXWWORKS, your COX is basically the purchase cost plus origin transportation and custom clearance plus international freight and destination transportation and custom clearance. So basically you look after all the custom clearances at both ends plus all the local transportation on both ends as well. So under FOB, which is the most, most commonly used method, most of the factories will quote based on FOB and they also, it's FOB to a particular port. Usually it's the closest port to the factory. So if the factory is located in Hong Kong, they will quote FOB Hong Kong price. Basically what that means is that they will produce the goods, they will help you ship all the way to Hong Kong port and they will include all the custom clearance at the end and also local transportation. And under FOB, your COX is very quite simple. It's basically the purchase cost plus the international shipping plus the destination transportation and custom clearance. And this is the most commonly used when you receive a quotation from a supplier and FOB is usually the term that you use. If not, you have to be super careful. And the next one is used for certain commodities, not for everything. It's called CIF or CNF. The COX is very, very even simpler. COX equals purchase cost plus your destination transportation and custom clearance. A lot of people might ask, why don't we get factory to quote CIF then, so that it's a lot easier. My answer to you is that not necessarily because they might not have the best rate for international transportation with the freight forward they're using. So we always go FOB, so we have the freedom to choose the cheapest carrier at that time. So these are the three different things and how it's affecting your COX. So when you calculate your COX with your products, you have to make sure that it's the right trading term that you're using. 90% of the time, the factory will quote based on FOB term because it's the easiest way for them to transact. It's also the easiest way for all the buyers as well. Certain factory will quote based on X-Works. On the surface, it might look cheaper, but in reality, it's going to be a lot more expensive for you because you're going to end up organizing local transportation at all regions. For example, if you're buying from China, you have to work with the freight forwarder to move the stock from factory to port, then move the stock from port to your destination port and all the way to your warehouse. So the quotation might be cheaper, but your COX might be more expensive.

## [06:08 - 09:08]

So basically the different terms are something that you can definitely request from the supplier and the supplier will quote a general price. You need to be very, very specific on the shipping term. You need to ask them if this X-Works or if this FOB and if they say this is X-Works, you need to ask them to include FOB chargers. So basically the quote should be FOB, not X-Works. And also, if you're trading on X-Works, most of the time they would accept local currency and not USD. And if you're doing FOB, it's almost 100%, it's USD. That's the differences. For factories, they're working with a lot of local transportation companies and doing a lot of customer clearance. It would be more efficient for them to do for you rather than you doing yourself. I would say 95% of the factories will quote you based on FOB because that's what they do. They've been doing this over the years, they're working with a lot of international clients. It's all quoting in FOB. If they're quoting X-Works, I would question the professionalism of this factory as well because they might not be a professional factory because being able to quote FOB and export, you need export license. And quoting in X-Works sometimes means they don't have export license. So when I first started my own business, I was sourcing a particular product and I got quotes from multiple factories. And one of the factories, like providing good quality products at very, very cheap rate. And I didn't realize it was based on X-Works. And I bought it, I had to move freight within China myself. And the local transportation cost actually killed my entire margin that I didn't realize because I had the X-Works price and I quoted my client based on that price. And the local transportation was definitely something I didn't anticipate. And that was a painful story. And from there, I pay extra attention to all the quotes I receive from manufacturers. One thing to note here is that FOB cost, FOB quote that you receive from the factory is a lot of time is based on a 24 container or 44 container load. And if you're shipping half a container load, the factory might be charging you like a surcharge. This could happen. So just something to be mindful of. So from FOB all the way to COX, how do you calculate it? A lot of people might ask the question. It's actually very simple. There are three main components to this calculation. The first one is manufacturing cost, which is how much did you pay under FOB?

## [09:08 - 11:38]

It can be $100, for example. The second one is transportation cost. Like how much did it cost me to transport one item from factory all the way to my warehouse? So that cost you can get from your freight forwarder, for example. A container cost might be $2,000. You're shipping 2,000 units inside. That means $1 each. So that means it's $101 for my production cost as well as my transportation cost. And on top of that, there's a duty cost. Duty cost is basically category by category, country by country. They're all different. So those are the three big components when you're calculating from FOB to COX. Don't get too stressed if you get a quote on the FILX works, because it works very, very similarly. So what you need to do is basically you need to find a freight forwarder that can help you move freight from the factory to the origin port and all the way to your warehouse. You need to make sure that either the supplier or the freight forwarder can have the export permit for the particular product that you're exporting. There is a little bit extra work, extra checks, but it's definitely not a mission impossible. I've got quoted on the X-Works terms a ton of times, and sometimes I just manage to get a factory to quote me back in FOB. So it's generally the manufacturer in charge of quoting the different trading terms, either X-Works or FOB or even CRF. And one thing to note is that people who generally quoting X-Works are generally smaller factories, and you just need to be super careful if they have the right export license. And what is the reason why they can't quote FOB? And if they can, I always encourage you to change it to FOB term.
